headline: The World Cup built a fortress. Now it has to fill it.
deck: The expanded tournament opened across three nations — a record three red cards in Mexico, a US rout, a Canadian point — behind the largest security operation in its history, and to visible empty seats. The interesting part is the gap between the two. Spain is the favourite; access is the story.
body: The football arrived first. Mexico beat South Africa 2–0 in an opener that produced a record three red cards and "descended into chaos"; the United States routed Paraguay 2–0 on home soil; Canada drew 1–1 with Bosnia for a creditable point; South Korea came back to beat Czechia 2–1. [E1] The bookmakers are unmoved by the hosts: Spain opens as narrow favourite around +450 — an implied ~18% — with France level and the United States a +5000 afterthought despite the win. [E5] The full title odds run in the ledger. The trophy is the easy forecast.

The hard one is whether the tournament works as an event. This is the first three-nation World Cup, staged behind a security footprint of 400-plus agencies and a quarter-billion dollars of anti-drone hardware. [E2] And yet seats are empty: reports describe roughly 176,000 unsold across the early phase and visible gaps at SoFi Stadium for the US opener. A tournament can be both the most secured in history and short of the crowds it was sold on, and that contradiction is the story.

Why the seats are empty is two separate questions, and the viral phrase circulating among fans — "a World Cup for them, not us" — unhelpfully welds them together. One is price: corporate and hospitality allocations against ordinary supporters, the ESPN tally of concerns leading with price-gouging. [E4] The other is the border: the BBC reports fan anger over US travel restrictions affecting supporters from more than a quarter of participating nations, with the expedited FIFA fan-visa programme drawing only tens of thousands of applications. [E3] Different causes, different fixes. Lumping them into one slogan about elites just hides that.

The narrow version is this: a host can run the largest security operation in tournament history and still keep people out, because the same visa and pricing posture that defines its border policy now decides who fills the stands. That is the access paradox of a fortress hosting a festival — an empirical gap between a 'safest-ever' logistics claim and the turnstile count, not a verdict on who deserves a ticket. The matches will resolve themselves. Whether the stadiums fill is the open question — so watch the turnstile count, not the grievance.

evidence_box.fragment: Opening results: Mexico 2–0 South Africa (three red cards), USA 2–0 Paraguay, Canada 1–1 Bosnia, South Korea 2–1 Czechia.
Coverage of the 2026 World Cup's geopolitical tensions: security operation spanning 400+ agencies and anti-drone technology, against suppressed international attendance.
Fan anger over US travel restrictions affecting supporters from more than a quarter of participating nations; low uptake of the expedited fan-visa programme.
An analyst's tally of tournament concerns: price-gouging, safety, visa red tape, and polarisation, with counterarguments on preparation.
World Cup title odds: Spain narrow favourite around +450 (implied ~18%), France level, USA a longshot near +5000.
